did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically an Inception CNN model, to extract discriminant features from dermoscopy images for skin lesion classification (melanoma detection). It also employs a window distance-controlled entropy feature selection method and neural networks for the final classification task on datasets like PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel integrated framework for skin lesion detection and recognition, detailing specific methodological contributions including saliency valuation, Gaussian contrast enhancement, Inception CNN feature extraction, and window distance-controlled entropy feature selection. It reports original experimental results, including accuracy metrics (97.74%, 96.1%, 97%) on public datasets (PH2, ISBI 2016, ISBI 2017), and compares performance against existing methods, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: An integrated framework of skin lesion detection and recognition through saliency method and optimal deep neural network features selection
brief_summary: This study proposes an integrated framework for automated skin lesion detection and recognition to address the challenges of early malignant melanoma diagnosis. The methodology employs a Gaussian contrast enhancement technique and color space transformation to construct saliency maps for lesion segmentation. Deep features are extracted using an Inception CNN model and fused via a decision-controlled parallel fusion method before being selected through a window distance-controlled entropy approach. The system was evaluated on three public datasets: PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017. The proposed method achieved classification accuracies of 97.74%, 96.1%, and 97% on these respective datasets. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms existing methods in terms of performance metrics.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on malignant melanoma detection and classification. The term 'malignant' is used as the class label in the datasets (ISBI 2016, ISBI 2017) and for the binary classification task against benign lesions.

Used as the comparator class in the binary classification task (benign vs. malignant). The datasets contain benign images (e.g., 2223 benign images in ISBI 2017) which serve as the negative class for melanoma detection.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions (melanoma). The system extracts deep features using an Inception CNN and classifies them using a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) neural network. Performance is reported as accuracy (97.74% on PH2, 96.1% on ISBI 2016, 97% on ISBI 2017), sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.

The framework includes a preprocessing step for lesion segmentation using a proposed saliency method (Gaussian contrast enhancement and color space transformation) to separate the foreground lesion from the background. Segmentation performance is evaluated using Dice rate and error rate on PH2 and ISBI 2016 datasets.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset used for validation. It contains images with ground truth for segmentation.

use.validation: Used for validating the proposed saliency-based segmentation method (Dice rate and negative rate) and for classification accuracy evaluation (97.74%).

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISBI 2016 Challenge on Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection. It contains RGB images with ground truth for segmentation.

use.validation: Used for validating the proposed saliency-based segmentation method (Dice rate and error rate) and for classification accuracy evaluation (96.1%).

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISBI 2017 Challenge. It contains RGB dermoscopy images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC, and other metrics without statistical significance testing.

segmentation.notes: Average Dice rate of 94.3239% and average negative rate (FNR) of 5.6761% computed for all 200 images. Maximum Dice rate of 96.75% reported.

classification.notes: The proposed method achieved the highest accuracy of 97.74% using Neural Network with selected features. Comparison with state-of-the-art shows improvement over Khan et al. [2] (97.5% accuracy).

segmentation.notes: Average Dice rate of 94.9227% and average error rate of 5.0773% computed for all images. Maximum Dice rate of 97.9448% reported.

classification.notes: The proposed method achieved the highest accuracy of 96.1% using Neural Network with selected features. Comparison with state-of-the-art shows improvement over Khan et al. [2] (83.2% accuracy).
The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 97.0%. Comparison with state-of-the-art shows improvement over Oliveira et al. [54] (94.3% accuracy) and Khan et al. [2] (88.2% accuracy).

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained deep learning model (specifically mentioned as 'pre-trained VGG-19' in one context but 'Inception V3' in the methodology and results sections; the primary model used for feature extraction in the proposed framework is Inception V3)

training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7 3770 CPU 3.40 GHz, 16 GB RAM, Nvidia GeForce GTX 980 4 GB GPU

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast stretching using a Gaussian distribution-based fitness function to enhance local contrast
Color space transformation from RGB to HSV
Saliency map construction using inner and outer disjoint windows on the high-intensity HSV channel
Image resizing to a fixed dimension of 512x512 pixels
conclusions: The proposed integrated framework, combining saliency-based segmentation with optimal deep neural network feature selection, effectively automates the detection and recognition of malignant melanoma.
The method achieved high classification accuracies of 97.74% on the PH2 dataset, 96.1% on the ISBI 2016 dataset, and 97% on the ISBI 2017 dataset.
The proposed saliency segmentation method outperformed existing methods, achieving average Dice rates of 94.32% on PH2 and 94.92% on ISBI 2016.

Using the window distance-controlled entropy feature selection method with a Neural Network classifier yielded superior performance compared to using color, geometric, or fused features alone, as well as other classifiers like SVM and KNN.
The proposed approach outperformed state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity across all three tested datasets.
The inclusion of a robust preprocessing step (Gaussian contrast enhancement and HSV color space transformation) significantly improved both segmentation and classification accuracy.
The optimal feature selection approach reduced computational complexity and recognition time while maintaining high classification accuracy.

Limitations include the method's dependence on the preprocessing step, the additional computational cost introduced by segmentation, and potential performance degradation when the number of predictors is low, particularly for complex dermoscopic images.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on three public datasets (PH2, ISBI 2016, ISBI 2017) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

The authors explicitly note limitations including dependency on the preprocessing step (contrast stretching), increased computational cost due to segmentation, and potential performance degradation when the number of predictors is low.
The PH2 dataset contains a small number of melanoma cases (40 out of 200), which may limit the robustness of the model for this specific class compared to larger datasets like ISBI 2017.
The paper does not report data leakage risks or detailed cross-validation protocols, relying instead on reported accuracy metrics from the datasets' standard splits or challenges.
The method's reliance on a saliency-based segmentation step introduces a potential failure point; errors in segmentation could propagate to feature extraction and classification stages.
